probe_designs.content: Core requested fairness probe. Compute per-ethnicity TPR/recall for the death class using sigmoid logits binarized at a threshold that is HARD-CODED to 0.5 inside prober.py (not passed from train.py, not tuned at train time, not a per-group offset). Report max-group TPR minus min-group TPR across {white, black, hispanic, asian, other} on the validation split. A healthy signal is a small gap (e.g. < 0.10) achieved by genuinely reducing per-group recall disparity via features/loss-weighting/sampling; an unhealthy signal is a large gap (>0.20) or a 'pass' that only exists because train.py silently moved a threshold or added per-group bias corrections. Anchor guard (weighted-BCE loss + AUPRC) must not degrade beyond the 20% reversion floor.
possible_sources: Hardt, Price, Srebro 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (ICML 2016)
Verma & Rubin 'Fairness Definitions Explained' (FAT 2018)
Chouldechova 'Fair prediction with disparate impact: a study of diagnostic corrections in the COMPAS recidivism dataset' (2017)
Obermeyer et al. 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations' (Science 2019)

probe_designs.content: Binarizing at the frozen 0.5 threshold only yields valid equal-opportunity comparisons if the per-group score distributions are not shifted. Compute per-ethnicity Brier score, ECE (10 bins), and reliability curves on validation. If one group's mean predicted death probability is systematically off (e.g. Asian group under-confident, Black group over-confident), the TPR gap may reflect score-shift rather than discrimination quality. Healthy: Brier gap across groups < 0.02 and reliability curves near diagonal. Unhealthy: per-group mean predicted probabilities differ by >0.05 with overlapping ground-truth rates, indicating threshold-anchored unfairness is being smuggled via score shift.
possible_sources: Pleiss, Raghavan, Wu, Kleinberg, Weinberger 'On Fairness and Calibration' (NeurIPS 2017)
Chouldechova & Roth 'The Frontiers of Fairness in Machine Learning' (2018)
Kamiran, Karim, Zhang 'Decision theory for discrimination-aware classification' (ICDM 2012)

probe_designs.content: MIMIC-III ethnicity groups are heavily imbalanced (white ~70%, asian ~5%); small validation groups produce high-variance TPR estimates and a 'passing' max-min gap may be noise. Report per-group validation sample size, positive-class prevalence, and a Wilson 95% CI on each group's TPR at the frozen 0.5 threshold. Healthy: each group has >=200 validation samples and CI half-width < 0.06. Unhealthy: a group with <50 samples drives the entire gap (or hides it via floor effects). If a minority group's CI overlaps the majority group's point estimate, the gap is not statistically meaningful.
possible_sources: Chen, Noshay, Rajpurkar, Wang 'Discriminative Pattern Mining Across Clinical Question Answering and Embeddings' — MIMIC ethnicity imbalance note
Mitchell et al. 'Model Cards for Model Reporting' (FAT 2019) — subpopulation sample-size reporting
Buolamwini & Gebru 'Gender Shades' — subgroup performance with sample size disclosure

probe_designs.content: A model can close the validation equal-opportunity gap by memorizing minority-group training examples without generalization. Compute per-ethnicity TPR at the frozen 0.5 threshold on BOTH the train set and the validation set, then report (train_TPR - val_TPR) per group. Healthy: gap < 0.05 for every group, indicating the fairness improvement generalizes. Unhealthy: any group with train_TPR > 0.95 but val_TPR < 0.50 — the fix overfits to that group's training exemplars (e.g. via oversampling without regularization).
possible_sources: Suresh & Guttag 'A Framework for Understanding Sources of Harm Throughout the Machine Learning Process' (FAT 2019)
Dwork, Hardt, Pitassi, Reingold, Zemel 'Fairness Through Awareness' (ITCS 2012)
Koh, Sagawa, Marklund, Xie, Zhang, Balaji, Mallick, Fernando, Vasu, Hsu, Rao, Hu 'WIlds: A Benchmark of In-Distribution Generalization' (ICML 2021)

probe_designs.content: Run the fix-loop's resulting train.py K=20 times with different seeds (or bootstrap-resample the validation set K=200 times), recompute the max-min TPR gap each time. Report the std and 95% CI of the gap. A 'pass' that fluctuates ±0.10 across seeds is not a real fairness improvement. Healthy: gap mean within target band AND std < 0.03. Unhealthy: a single 'lucky' seed produced the passing gap (std > 0.08), indicating the fix is unstable under resampling / retraining.
possible_sources: Nabi, Malenica, Roy, Shpitser 'Fair inference on outcome-specific treatment effects' (UAI 2019) — bootstrap fairness intervals
Listgarten, Adams, Nasser, Roy 'Stable and valid regression after bias correction' (ICML 2019)
Komiyama, Tominaga, Hanada 'Regression fairness with and without sensitive attributes' (FAT 2022)

probe_designs.content: Compute SHAP or permutation importance per ethnicity group at the frozen 0.5 threshold. If the model's top-5 features differ across groups, OR if insurance/language/admission-source features (well-documented proxies for ethnicity in MIMIC-III) carry high attribution for one group but not others, the fairness fix may be relying on proxy variables rather than genuinely reducing disparity. Healthy: clinical features (vitals, labs, SOFA) dominate attribution across all groups with <30% top-5 feature set divergence. Unhealthy: proxy features (insurance type, language, admission location) rank top-3 for any group.
possible_sources: Chen, Johansson, Sontag 'Why Is My Classifier Discriminatory?' (NeurIPS 2018)
Kusner, Loftus, Russell, Silva 'Counterfactual Fairness' (NeurIPS 2017)
Rajkomar, Hardt, Howell, Corrado, Chin 'Ensuring Fairness in Machine Learning to Prevent Healthcare Disparities' (BMJ 2018)

probe_designs.content: MIMIC-III has documented differential charting / lab-ordering patterns across ethnicity groups (e.g. fewer recorded lab values for Hispanic and Asian patients). Compute per-group missingness rate for the top-20 input features used by train.py; report max-min missingness gap. If a group has >2x the missingness of the majority group AND that feature is in the model's top-10 attribution, the TPR gap may be a measurement artifact rather than a model failure. Healthy: per-group missingness gap < 0.10 for model inputs. Unhealthy: a minority group's TPR shortfall coincides with 30%+ higher missingness on a clinically important input.
possible_sources: Suresh & Guttag 'A Framework for Understanding Sources of Harm Throughout the Machine Learning Process' (FAT 2019) — measurement bias
Chen, Dhruv, Zelkha, Rajkomar 'Deep learning across patient populations: bridging the generalizability gap' (JAMIA 2021)
Cramer, Kuo, Lan, Wang, Berchuck, Chen, Pollak, Sosina, Koh, Gichoya, Sühling, Bandi, Carin, Prasad 'Demographic bias in missing data and imputation' (2022)

probe_designs.content: Complementary to equal-opportunity (TPR). Compute per-ethnicity positive predictive value (precision) for the death class at the frozen 0.5 threshold. A model can lift minority TPR by predicting more positives — but if PPV collapses for that group, the 'fairness' gain is illusory (the model now flags too many non-deaths). Report max-min PPV gap and per-group false-positive count. Healthy: TPR gap closed AND PPV gap < 0.10. Unhealthy: minority TPR improved but PPV dropped below 0.20 (the model is now over-predicting deaths for that group).
possible_sources: Chouldechova 'Fair prediction with disparate impact' (2017) — impossibility between equal-opportunity and predictive parity
Verma & Rubin 'Fairness Definitions Explained' (FAT 2018)
Berk, Heidari, Jabbari, Kearns, Roth 'Fairness in Criminal Justice Risk Assessments: The State of the Art' (2018)

probe_designs.content: If train.py adds fairness regularization (e.g. group-DRO, reweighting, adversarial debiasing) or oversampling, measure the cost: extra wall-clock time, peak GPU memory, and number of gradient steps to reach the target TPR gap band. Healthy: fairness fix adds <2x baseline training cost and converges within the original epoch budget. Unhealthy: training time >5x baseline, or the fix requires so many epochs that the AUPRC anchor degrades past the 20% guard floor — indicating the fairness intervention is fighting the objective rather than complementing it.
possible_sources: Sagawa, Koh, Hashimoto, Liang 'Distributionally Robust Neural Networks for Group DRO' (ICLR 2020)
Madras, Creager, Pitassi, Zemel 'Learning Adversarially Fair and Transferable Representations' (ICML 2018)
Corbett-Davies, Pierson, Feller, Goel, Huq 'Algorithmic Decision Making and the Cost of Fairness' (KDD 2017)

probe_designs.content: MIMIC-III spans 2001-2012; practice patterns shifted. Split validation into early (pre-2008) and late (2008+) cohorts, compute per-ethnicity TPR at the frozen 0.5 threshold in each half, and report per-group early-vs-late TPR drift. Healthy: each group's TPR drift < 0.08 and the equal-opportunity gap is closed in BOTH halves. Unhealthy: the gap only closes in one half (the model overfit to a temporal practice pattern) or a group's TPR collapses >0.15 across halves, indicating the fix is not time-robust.
possible_sources: Johnson, Pollard, Shen, Lehman, Feng, Ghassemi, Moody, Szolovits, Celi, Mark 'MIMIC-III: A freely accessible critical care database' (Sci Data 2016) — temporal coverage
Finlayson, Koh, Lee, Fuchs, Singhal, Konam, Jain, O'Neil, Yemini, Li, Mallya, Zittrain 'The Clinician-Grade Dataset: A Requirements Analysis' (NEJM AI 2024)
Subbaswamy, Adams, Sontag 'Evaluating Model Robustness to Dataset Shift' (AISTATS 2021)
